Bill? Doug Laser still operate my original marketing company which is BGS Marketing and also operate Glaser Kennedy Insider Circle which we publish for newsletters. We have four different coaching programs, a website, a web store. We put on two major seminars a year and a couple minor, less major seminars a year. And I have an area exclusive program which Chris is an IVA of. And probably if I think about it I'll get too depressed for the other stuff we do. So turn it over to the Mr. Information Marketing Association. Thank you. Robert Scrobe with the Information Marketing Association, really the association guy for info marketers. And I've kind of given up being likable and influential in person so I figure I'll try it in writing. That's the old joke. You have a face perfect for radio, right? I'm Bill Harrison and my brother Steve and I help authors and others get free publicity on radio TV shows and help authors market their books. I'm here to learn to write more like Dan. I just finished a three day seminar. I'm exhausted. You know how you think, oh geez, can I really, should I just not get on the airplane? I said this would be like not getting a chance to hear Billy Joel talk about songwriting for three days if you want to be a songwriter. I mean Dan does this so well that I'm just really looking forward to it. Steve Harrison. Hope you remember. I'm Eric Ruth and I'm here with my partner Sean Greeley. We both run a company called NetProfit Explosion. We have an air exclusive and coaching program and we sell information products to the fitness industry, the personal trainers and studio owners and such. We're both here to get better, faster, stronger and excited to be here to become more important. We put out two newsletters and a ton of other writing, a lot of copies on the web and a snail mail. So excited to be here. Well above everything else, pretty much the commonality in everybody's questionnaires, those who filled them out, is that it should be no surprise, is that really what everybody would like is a couple of checklists and some fill-in-the-blank templates. You know to make this really easy. And if I could figure out how to charge this much money and just mail you the checklist and the envelope and get away with it, I would have done so. But there are two essentially that you're going to get. So you will see in your notebook, we're going to go through 31 principles or strategies, whatever you wish to call them. That ultimately comprise a checklist of sorts because everything that you do in print, the more of the 31 that it hits, the better. And then tomorrow, sometime, I'll give you my newsletter checklist. Not that it should be necessarily your newsletter checklist, but I'll take you through mine so you see the foundation on which I try and build mine. And ultimately, you will want to keep some of those items, you'll want to create your own. But although there are a couple of people who are very closely, of course, following ours by observation only, and that's okay too. So you will leave with two actual detailed checklists that can be used every time you write and can be stuck up on the bullet board and so forth. Because I know that obviously is really what everybody wants. And I'm going to talk you through the 31 principles, some of which I have a lot to say about, some of which I don't have a lot to say about. They're sort of pretty much self-explanatory. And as we go, I did warn everybody this was workshop, not lecture. So there are a crapload of exercises, most of which you will do by moving to the little roundtables and doing as groups. Only a couple are sort of traditional writing exercises. The rest are actually build-a-bear kind of exercises, meaning that the outcome of them is something you can take with you and use when you get home. And so you will be building resources, not just having experiences to learn from, but you'll actually be working on your stuff and working on your businesses and building resources that you will take with you that will have value after you've left the room. So it's not the normal busywork kind of workshop, which has its value, but where everybody gets divided up in groups and you play, if there were only four people left in a boat and you had to throw one of them overboard, which one of the four would it be, those kinds of things. These actually are intended for you to walk out of here with additional checklists and additional tools and additional resources that will have some value to you after you leave. The one thing I would ask so I don't have to play a school marm is that you'll really get most value if you don't divide up into little groups and stay in the same group for the three days. It will be helpful if you swap yourselves out from exercise to exercise. I am not going to run around the room and make that happen. So I'll rely on you to be big people and kind of do that for yourselves. Everybody plays, by the way. So, and as we go, that doesn't mean I won't table it, but you should completely feel free to speak up to make this discussion, not me talking for long uninterrupted periods of time. And I may or may not deal with it when it comes up, but your samples, those who sent them in, I sifted through them and picked stuff to put in that spiral bound book. Those of you who did not send them in and brought them with you, that's fine. If you have enough to pass out to everybody, we'll do that. If you don't, that's okay too. You will want them for some of the exercises as reference materials for yourself. John, your box of catalogs is over here and somebody else's stuff is over there. And you'll want that with you. Ultimately, we will kind of quick hot seat almost everybody, probably the third day, because I have notes on everybody's materials. But by the time we get there, I suspect you'll be able to really do your own hot seats and kind of say, okay, here's where we are and here's now what I think I want to indeed to change in order to make what I'm doing more effective. And so you will find on page four in your notebook, we have numbered pages. You will find, I'll show you a couple of very funny things about this notebook, which comes from having cheap unsupervised staff, but bad combination, by the way, cheap and unsupervised. But on page four, you'll find a list of the 31 principles, which will ultimately, they'll all mean something to you by the time we get out of here. So this actually will be a useful checklist.
